content.text: Why Conversation-Level Cost Is the Right Unit

Token bills arrive as a single aggregate per model. Attaching that spend to business outcomes — a resolved support ticket, a drafted document, a reviewed pull request — requires mapping raw token counts through turns, sessions, and tasks. Cost-per-conversation is the primary FinOps KPI for interactive GenAI workloads because it bridges the billing layer (tokens) to the product layer (user sessions).
Data Model: Tokens → Turns → Conversations → Tasks
The hierarchy has four levels. Each level aggregates the one below it. Capturing the right fields at each level enables drill-down when a budget alert fires.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class LLMCall:
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    latency_ms: int
    call_type: str        # "chat" | "embedding" | "rerank" | "tool"
    timestamp: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)

@dataclass
class ConversationTurn:
    turn_id: str
    user_message: str
    llm_calls: list[LLMCall] = field(default_factory=list)

@dataclass
class Conversation:
    conversation_id: str
    user_id: str
    team: str
    feature: str          # "support-bot" | "code-assistant" | "doc-drafter"
    turns: list[ConversationTurn] = field(default_factory=list)
    resolved: Optional[bool] = None   # None = still open
    task_outcome: Optional[str] = None  # e.g. "ticket_closed"
A
turn
is one user message plus all LLM calls triggered by it. A single turn may include a retrieval call, a reranking call, and a generation call — all of those costs belong to that turn.
Cost Computation from Tokens
With per-model price tables and the data model above, costs roll up naturally. The price registry must handle versioning so historical reports remain accurate when a provider changes pricing.
Unit Metrics Report
Computing
cost_per_conversation_usd

alone misses the resolution dimension. A conversation that ends without resolving the user's problem costs the same as one that succeeds — but their business value differs. Tracking both the raw average and the resolved-conversation cost reveals efficiency gaps.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from statistics import mean, median

@dataclass
class UnitMetricsReport:
    feature: str
    period: str
    total_conversations: int
    resolved_conversations: int
    total_cost_usd: float
    cost_per_conversation_usd: float
    cost_per_resolved_conversation_usd: float
    cost_per_turn_usd: float
    avg_turns_per_conversation: float
    median_tokens_per_conversation: int
    p95_cost_usd: float    # 95th percentile — catch outliers

def compute_unit_metrics(
    conversations: list[Conversation],
    feature: str,
    period: str,
) -> UnitMetricsReport:
    feature_convs = [c for c in conversations if c.feature == feature]
    if not feature_convs:
        raise ValueError(f"No conversations for feature {feature!r}")

    costs = [conversation_cost_usd(c) for c in feature_convs]
    resolved = [c for c in feature_convs if c.resolved is True]
    resolved_costs = [conversation_cost_usd(c) for c in resolved]

    all_turns = [t for c in feature_convs for t in c.turns]
    turn_costs = [turn_cost_usd(t) for t in all_turns]

    all_tokens = [
        sum(lc.input_tokens + lc.output_tokens for t in c.turns for lc in t.llm_calls)
        for c in feature_convs
    ]

    sorted_costs = sorted(costs)
    p95_idx = int(len(sorted_costs) * 0.95)

    return UnitMetricsReport(
        feature=feature,
        period=period,
        total_conversations=len(feature_convs),
        resolved_conversations=len(resolved),
        total_cost_usd=sum(costs),
        cost_per_conversation_usd=mean(costs),
        cost_per_resolved_conversation_usd=mean(resolved_costs) if resolved_costs else 0.0,
        cost_per_turn_usd=mean(turn_costs) if turn_costs else 0.0,
        avg_turns_per_conversation=mean(len(c.turns) for c in feature_convs),
        median_tokens_per_conversation=int(median(all_tokens)),
        p95_cost_usd=sorted_costs[p95_idx],
    )
Task-Level Unit Economics
Some features map one task to multiple conversations (a complex document draft may go through several chat sessions).
TaskUnitRecord
links aggregated conversation cost to the business outcome and the human time it displaces.
is_cost_effective

compares AI cost to 1% of saved human salary cost. If an AI task costs $0.10 and saves 15 minutes at $80/hr ($20), the ratio is 0.5% — well within the threshold. Adjust the 1% multiplier to your organisation's value-of-time calibration.
Reference Benchmarks
Feature
Typical Cost/Conv
Target Threshold
P95 Ceiling
Support chatbot
Code assistant
Document drafter
Data analyst agent
Multi-step research

Thresholds vary by feature value. A code review that saves 30 minutes of engineer time can justify a higher unit cost than a simple FAQ response. Calibrate targets from the task-ROI model, not from raw cost alone.
Outlier Detection

The P95 ceiling matters as much as the average. A runaway conversation — one where a user pastes an entire codebase into the chat — can generate 10× the typical token count. Detecting and capping these protects both budget and latency.
Persisting Metrics to a Database

In-memory computation works during a request but metrics must persist for trending, alerting, and chargeback. A lightweight SQLite schema is sufficient for teams under ~10 M conversations/month; migrate to a columnar store (BigQuery, Redshift) beyond that.

import sqlite3
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def get_db(path: str = "unit_metrics.db"):
    conn = sqlite3.connect(path)
    conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row
    try:
        yield conn
    finally:
        conn.close()

def create_schema(path: str = "unit_metrics.db"):
    with get_db(path) as db:
        db.executescript("""
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS conversation_costs (
                conversation_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                user_id         TEXT NOT NULL,
                team            TEXT NOT NULL,
                feature         TEXT NOT NULL,
                period          TEXT NOT NULL,   -- YYYY-MM
                total_cost_usd  REAL NOT NULL,
                turn_count      INTEGER NOT NULL,
                token_count     INTEGER NOT NULL,
                resolved        INTEGER,          -- 0/1/NULL
                recorded_at     TEXT NOT NULL
            );
            CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_cc_feature_period
                ON conversation_costs(feature, period);
        """)
        db.commit()

def upsert_conversation_cost(
    db: sqlite3.Connection,
    conv: Conversation,
) -> None:
    cost = conversation_cost_usd(conv)
    turns = len(conv.turns)
    tokens = sum(
        lc.input_tokens + lc.output_tokens
        for t in conv.turns for lc in t.llm_calls
    )
    period = datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y-%m")
    db.execute("""
        INSERT INTO conversation_costs
            (conversation_id, user_id, team, feature, period,
             total_cost_usd, turn_count, token_count, resolved, recorded_at)
        VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)
        ON CONFLICT(conversation_id) DO UPDATE SET
            total_cost_usd = excluded.total_cost_usd,
            turn_count     = excluded.turn_count,
            token_count    = excluded.token_count,
            resolved       = excluded.resolved
    """, (
        conv.conversation_id, conv.user_id, conv.team, conv.feature,
        period, cost, turns, tokens,
        (1 if conv.resolved else 0) if conv.resolved is not None else None,
        datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    ))
    db.commit()

def query_unit_metrics_sql(
    db: sqlite3.Connection,
    feature: str,
    period: str,
) -> dict:
    row = db.execute("""
        SELECT
            COUNT(*)                      AS n_convs,
            SUM(total_cost_usd)           AS total_cost,
            AVG(total_cost_usd)           AS avg_cost,
            AVG(turn_count)               AS avg_turns,
            CAST(AVG(token_count) AS INT) AS avg_tokens,
            SUM(resolved)                 AS n_resolved
        FROM conversation_costs
        WHERE feature = ? AND period = ?
    """, (feature, period)).fetchone()
    return dict(row)
Interpreting the Metrics Dashboard
Rising avg cost, stable resolution rate
— model regression or prompt bloat. Diff recent prompts.
Falling avg cost, falling resolution rate
— over-aggressive optimisation (cheaper model, less context). Check CSAT.
High P95 relative to mean
— unbounded input from users. Add token budget per turn.
Cost per resolved conv diverging from cost per conv
— resolution rate dropping. Investigate clarification loops.
Task cost rising without volume change
— model price increase or prompt expansion. Trigger price-impact analysis.
Alerting Thresholds in Practice

Wire the metrics into your observability stack. A useful alert hierarchy: warn at 2× target, page at 5× target, circuit-break at 10× target per conversation. This prevents a single pathological user session from draining a team's monthly budget.
Summary

Cost-per-conversation is the unit that connects token billing to product economics. Computing it requires a four-level hierarchy (token → turn → conversation → task), per-model price tables with versioning, and aggregation functions that separate resolved from unresolved sessions. Outlier detection at P95 prevents single heavy users from distorting the team average. Persisting the metrics to a queryable store enables trending, chargeback, and alert integration — turning raw billing data into an operational FinOps signal.
